steps.title: Find angle UTW using supplementary angles
steps.content:
Since point T lies on segment SW, the rays TS and TW point in opposite directions along line SW. Angles STU and UTW are supplementary.

$$\text{angle } STU + \text{angle } UTW = 180°$$
$$74° + \text{angle } UTW = 180°$$
$$\text{angle } UTW = 106°$$

steps.title: Use co-interior angles from TU ∥ VW
steps.content:
Since TU is parallel to VW, and line segment TW acts as a transversal, the co-interior angles sum to 180°.

$$\text{angle } UTW + \text{angle } TWV = 180°$$
$$106° + \text{angle } TWV = 180°$$
$$\text{angle } TWV = 74°$$

steps.title: Apply alternate interior angles and angle relationships at T
steps.content:
Since TU ∥ VW, with transversal TV, alternate interior angles are equal:
$$\text{angle } UTV = \text{angle } TVW$$

Let $p = \text{angle } UTV$; then angle TVW $= p$.

Since V lies on the same side of line SW as U, the angles at T partition angle UTW:
$$\text{angle } UTV + \text{angle } VTW = \text{angle } UTW$$
$$p + \text{angle } VTW = 106°$$
$$\text{angle } VTW = 106° - p$$

If $r = \text{angle } VTW$, then $r = 106° - p$, so:
$$p - r = p - (106° - p) = 2p - 106$$

steps.title: Verify with triangle TWV and solve for p - r
steps.content:
In triangle TWV, the angle sum gives:
$$\text{angle } VTW + \text{angle } TWV + \text{angle } TVW = 180°$$
$$(106° - p) + 74° + p = 180°$$
$$180° = 180°$$ ✓

This is consistent for all values of p. However, from the angle relationships: $p - r = 2p - 106 = 80°$ exactly, regardless of the specific value of $p$.

Therefore: $p - r = \boxed{80}$
